LO 42E · Brain Development

Brain Development Through Week 5

From a flat neural plate to a recognizable brain with five distinct regions — all before the end of the first month and a half of life.

🗺 The Big Picture
The entire brain starts as a flat sheet of ectoderm. That sheet folds into a tube, the tube balloons into three bubbles, those bubbles subdivide into five, and each bubble becomes a specific adult brain region. Simultaneously, the growing tube bends into dramatic curves called flexures. Everything here happens between day 18 and the end of week 5.
1
Week 3
Neural plate → neural groove → neural tube closes. CNS is born.
2
Week 4 (~day 28)
Three primary vesicles: Prosencephalon, Mesencephalon, Rhombencephalon.
3
Week 5 (~day 35)
Five secondary vesicles — each will become a distinct adult brain region.
4
Flexures
Three bends shape the geometry of the brainstem and open the 4th ventricle.
5
Later (Wks 6–38)
Telencephalon grows in a C-arc; cortex gains gyri progressively from week 26.

Master Timeline

Day 18
Neural plate forms
Day 19–21
Neural groove + folds
Day 22–23
Folds begin fusing
Day 28
3 Primary Vesicles
Day 29–30
Flexures visible
Day 32–35
5 Secondary Vesicles
Wk 8–14
C-shape growth
Wk 26–38
Gyri & sulci form
FIGURE 1Days 15–21: neural plate → groove → folds
ADULT ANATOMYInferior view — brainstem color-coded for orientation
ADULT ANATOMYSagittal view — major subdivisions labeled
Step 1 · Week 3

① Neural Tube Formation

Everything starts with ectoderm getting a signal. That signal triggers the entire central nervous system.

📋Day 18Neural PlateNotochord induces overlying ectoderm to thicken into neuroectoderm — the neural plate
🌊Day 19–20Neural GrooveMidline invaginates; raised edges become neural folds on either side
🤝Day 22–23Folds FuseFusion begins at the cervical region and zips both cranially and caudally
🔒Day 25–28Tube SealedAnterior neuropore closes ~day 25; posterior neuropore closes ~day 27–28
🧪SimultaneousNeural CrestAs folds fuse, neural crest cells delaminate from the tips and migrate — cranial nerve ganglia, craniofacial bone/cartilage, and more
⚠️ Why Closure Matters
Anterior neuropore failure → anencephaly (fatal). Posterior failure → spina bifida. Folic acid 400–800 µg/day started before conception reduces NTD risk by ~70%.

What the Tube Walls Become

Even early on, the neural tube walls are not uniform. There is an alar plate (dorsal) and a basal plate (ventral), separated by the sulcus limitans.

Alar plate (dorsal)→Sensory nuclei(afferent — receives input)
Basal plate (ventral)→Motor nuclei(efferent — sends output)
Sulcus limitans— groove dividing alar from basal plate; visible in spinal cord and hindbrain
FIGURE 1Neural plate (day 18) → groove → folds → closure
FIGURE 6Alar/basal plates & 4th ventricle opening from pontine flexure
Step 2 · ~Week 4, Day 28

② Three Primary Vesicles

Once the neural tube seals, the cranial end rapidly expands into three distinct bulges — like three balloons inflating at the head of a straw.

💡 Key Concept
Only the cranial end of the neural tube inflates into vesicles. The caudal remainder stays narrow and becomes the spinal cord. Each vesicle has a wall (future brain tissue) and a lumen (future ventricular cavity).
🔴Most AnteriorProsencephalonForebrain. Optic vesicles bud off here (~day 28). Will subdivide into telencephalon + diencephalon.
🟡MiddleMesencephalonMidbrain. The only primary vesicle that doesn't subdivide — stays mesencephalon in the 5-vesicle stage too.
🔵Most PosteriorRhombencephalonHindbrain. Internally segmented into rhombomeres (Rh.1–7), each associated with specific cranial nerves.
📝 Mnemonic — 3 Primary Vesicles (anterior → posterior)
"Pros Make Rhymes" → Prosencephalon · Mesencephalon · Rhombencephalon
Or simply: Fore → Mid → Hind

What Are Rhombomeres?

The rhombencephalon isn't a smooth balloon — it's internally segmented into rhombomeres (Rh.1–7), each expressing specific Hox genes. This matters because cranial nerve motor nuclei arise from specific rhombomeres, and neural crest cells from each rhombomere migrate into specific pharyngeal arches (e.g., CN V from Rh.2–3; CN VII/VIII from Rh.4; CN IX from Rh.5–6).

FIGURE 2Day 29–30: primary vesicles visible; rhombomeres labeled
FIGURE 4Cranial nerves & rhombomere map at Day 32
Step 3 · Week 5, ~Day 32–35

③ Five Secondary Vesicles

By week 5, two of the three primary vesicles subdivide. These five vesicles map directly to the adult brain's major divisions.

The Subdivision

Prosencephalon→Telencephalon+Diencephalon
Mesencephalon→Mesencephalon(unchanged — doesn't subdivide)
Rhombencephalon→Metencephalon+Myelencephalon

Adult Derivatives — Complete Map

Primary (3°)Secondary (5°)Adult Structure (Wall)Ventricular Cavity
ProsencephalonTelencephalonCerebral hemispheres (cortex, basal ganglia, hippocampus, amygdala, olfactory bulbs)Lateral ventricles (L + R)
DiencephalonThalami, hypothalamus, epithalamus, subthalamus
+ retina/CN II evaginate from here		3rd ventricle
MesencephalonMesencephalonMidbrain (tectum, tegmentum, peduncles)
CN III & IV nuclei, red nucleus, substantia nigra		Cerebral aqueduct
RhombencephalonMetencephalonPons + Cerebellum
CN V, VI, VII, VIII nuclei		Upper 4th ventricle
MyelencephalonMedulla oblongata
CN IX–XII nuclei, pyramids, olivary nucleus		Lower 4th ventricle
👁 Special Case: Retina & CN II
The retina and optic nerve evaginate from the diencephalon wall as the optic vesicle — they are literally brain tissue. This is why CN II is myelinated by oligodendrocytes (not Schwann cells), enclosed by meninges, and why MS plaques can cause optic neuritis directly. Papilledema occurs because raised ICP transmits through the subarachnoid space that surrounds CN II.
📝 Mnemonic — 5 Secondary Vesicles (anterior → posterior)
"Telepathic Dinosaurs Measure Meters Meticulously" (T-D-M-M-M)
Telencephalon · Diencephalon · Mesencephalon · Metencephalon · Myelencephalon
FIGURE 3Brain Vesicles: 3 primary → 5 secondary → adult derivatives + ventricular system
Step 4

④ Brain Flexures

The brain grows faster than the skull — it has to fold. Three flexures define the shape of the developing brainstem and have lasting anatomical consequences.

📐First & LargestCephalic (Midbrain) FlexureBends ventrally at the mesencephalon. Brain tip folds toward the heart. First to appear (~day 28).
↩️Most PosteriorCervical FlexureBends ventrally at the hindbrain–spinal cord junction. Defines where brain ends and cord begins.
↪️CounterflexurePontine FlexureBends dorsally (opposite direction!) at the metencephalon. Opens the 4th ventricle roof like a diamond.
⭐ High-Yield: The Pontine Flexure Rearranges CN Nuclei

Because the pontine flexure bends dorsally, it stretches the roof plate and pulls the alar plates laterally away from the basal plates. In the spinal cord, sensory is dorsal and motor is ventral. In the hindbrain floor (4th ventricle), the arrangement becomes medial → lateral:

Most medial→GSE (motor to extraocular/tongue)
→ Lateral→SVE/GVE (pharyngeal arch motor; parasympathetic)
Most lateral→GVA/SVA/GSA/SSA (sensory — visceral, taste, somatic, hearing/balance)
FIGURE 5Brain Flexures — external appearance
FIGURE 6Internal results of flexures: CN column arrangement around 4th ventricle
Step 5

⑤ Telencephalic Growth — The C-Shape

The telencephalon grows so fast it can't go straight — it sweeps in a C-arc, and everything attached to it gets dragged along.

💡 Why It Matters
The C-shaped growth explains why so many adult brain structures are C-shaped: lateral ventricles, fornix, caudate nucleus, hippocampus, cingulate gyrus. When you see a C-shaped structure in neuro, ask: was it dragged along by telencephalic expansion?

Direction of Growth

Anterior→Superior→Posterior→Inferior & anterior again→ completes the C-arc

Key C-Shaped Structures

🌀VentriclesLateral VentricleFrontal, occipital, and temporal horns follow the C-arc
🐾Gray MatterCaudate NucleusIts tail follows the C-arc into the temporal lobe
🦢White MatterFornixArches over the thalamus from mammillary bodies → septal nuclei
🐠CortexHippocampusSweeps from septal area → temporal lobe (uncus)
🎀GyrusCingulate GyrusWraps around the corpus callosum in a C
🌊Buried CortexInsulaBuried by rapid growth of frontal/parietal/temporal opercula closing over it
FIGURE 7Telencephalic C-shape; lateral ventricle, interventricular foramen, 3rd ventricle
ADULT ANATOMYSagittal section: corpus callosum, fornix, and diencephalon in context
Step 6

⑥ Cortical Development

A premature brain at 20 weeks literally looks like a smooth ball. Gyri and sulci form progressively from week 26 — driven by the cortex outgrowing the skull.

Week 14
Smooth (lissencephalic)
Week 26
Central + lateral sulci appear; insula starts burying
Week 30
More gyri; temporal + occipital lobes defined
Week 38 (Term)
Full gyrification; insula fully buried

Inside-Out Neuronal Migration

Neurons are produced in the ventricular zone and migrate outward along radial glia. New neurons must travel past earlier arrivals, so cortical layers form inside-out:

Layer VI neurons — arrive first (shortest migration)
Layer II neurons — arrive last (must pass all others)

Disruptions to radial glial scaffolding cause lissencephaly (migration stops early) or heterotopia (islands of neurons arrested mid-migration).

🩺 Clinical — Lissencephaly
Caused by LIS1 mutations (impaired dynein → radial glia migration failure). Smooth brain, severe intellectual disability, epilepsy. Detectable on prenatal MRI after week 24.
FIGURE 8Cortical development: weeks 14, 26, 30, 38
Step 7 · Clinical Correlates

⑦ Clinical Applications

Each congenital brain anomaly maps to a specific step in development that went wrong.

Neural Tube Defects

🧠Anterior NTDAnencephalyAnterior neuropore fails to close. Forebrain & skull vault absent. Lethal.
🦴Skull DefectCranium BifidumSkull defect; contents vary from meninges only (meningocele) to brain + meninges (encephalocele).
🫧Meninges OnlyCranial MeningoceleBetter prognosis. Only CSF + meninges herniate; brain remains in situ.
🫁Brain + MeningesEncephaloceleBrain tissue herniates. Occipital location most common. Cerebellum often involved.
🧬Posterior NTDSpina BifidaPosterior neuropore failure. Ranges from occulta (asymptomatic) to myelomeningocele.
💊PreventionFolic Acid400–800 µg/day before conception. Reduces NTD risk ~70%.

Vesicle & Division Defects

🌐ProsencephalonHoloprosencephalyTelencephalon fails to divide into two hemispheres. Single midline ventricle + midline facial defects. Associated with trisomy 13.
🌊TelencephalonAgenesis of CCCorpus callosum fails to form. "Racing-car" shaped lateral ventricles on axial MRI.
🧩CortexLissencephalyFailed gyrification. LIS1 mutation. Severe ID + epilepsy.
🗺CortexCortical HeterotopiaNeurons arrest mid-migration. Focal epilepsy. Found on MRI during epilepsy workup.

Pituitary Formation (Two-Source Rule)

🏆 High-Yield Dual Origin
Oral ectoderm (Rathke's pouch)→Adenohypophysis(anterior pituitary: pars distalis, tuberalis, intermedia)
Diencephalon floor (infundibulum)→Neurohypophysis(posterior pituitary: pars nervosa + infundibular stalk)

Craniopharyngioma arises from Rathke's pouch remnants → suprasellar mass → visual field defects + hormonal dysfunction.

FIGURE 9Pituitary formation: Rathke's pouch + infundibulum
FIGURE 10Cranium Bifidum: occipital encephalocele + MRI
